data.name: Melvin Mastil
data.description: Most Algeria fans know very little about the French-born Mastil, who was called up only a few months before the World Cup, but he has long been a fan of the Fennecs. Mastil, who qualifies for Algeria through a grandfather, told L'Équipe: “I grew up supporting the Algerian national team. When I saw them at the 2014 World Cup and the 2019 Africa Cup of Nations, it made me dream.” Now he has the chance to build a long-term future with his grandad’s country. The 26-year-old has spent his entire professional career in Switzerland and has most recently been on loan in the second division with Stade Nyonnais. With Algeria in need of a refresh in the goalkeeping department, Mastil cuts an intriguing profile, standing at 1.93m (6ft 4in) and captaining his club. His potential ceiling appears higher than that of other Algerian goalkeepers in Europe, making him a strong candidate to serve as a reserve keeper for now.

data.name: Aïssa Mandi
data.description: The consummate professional, with more caps than any other Algerian, having passed the 100 mark in 2024. Perhaps his former coach with the national team Djamel Belmadi described him best when he said: “Aissa has natural leadership skills and a significant impact on the players, both in matches and in training. He’s a good person and a positive presence within the group. Even his lack of speed, he compensates for with intelligence and sound judgment when it comes to taking initiative.” Mandi is one of two veterans in this squad, alongside Riyad Mahrez, who was also in the 2014 World Cup squad that pushed Germany to its limit in the last 16. He has a big role to play this summer, on the pitch or off it, in being a calming, authoritative presence for the younger defenders. When Algeria qualified for this summer he wrote online: “Since 29 March 2022 only one thing has been on our minds: bringing Algeria back where it belongs: to the World Cup. Pride and determination for what comes next."

data.name: Achref Abada
data.description: A real late bloomer, Abada made his international breakthrough at the age of 26. For much of his career the centre-back went under the radar, playing in Algeria for MC El Eulma and ASO Chlef, but he really turned heads with his displays at last year’s Fifa Arab Cup in Qatar, impressing with his speed, athleticism and relentless aggression. In January he signed for USM Alger on a lucrative long-term deal, with the deal featuring a big sell-on clause and another clause reportedly stipulating that his new club would fund his old club’s pre-season training camp this summer if Abada made the World Cup squad. Good news for ASO Chlef, then. There are still areas for improvement when it comes to playing with the ball at his feet, but his defensive instincts alone earned him his call-up this summer.

data.name: Mohamed Amine Tougai
data.description: Algeria did not announce their World Cup squad until 31 May, significantly later than most of the participating nations. By then, there were few surprises left. Journalists stationed outside the airport had already photographed and filmed players arriving for camp, gradually piecing together Vladimir Petkovic's squad before the official announcement. The notable exception was Tougai. The defender had returned to Algeria several weeks earlier from Tunisia, where his club endured a disappointing campaign and failed to win the league title for only the second time in the past decade. His inclusion raised eyebrows, particularly as Petkovic has selected six centre-backs for the tournament. Among them, Tougai is arguably the least versatile. A traditional, no-nonsense defender, he prioritises winning physical duels and clearing danger over building attacks or playing line-breaking passes through midfield.

data.name: Zineddine Belaïd
data.description: A reliable, if unspectacular, centre-back. Belaid’s greatest achievement came in 2023 when he captained USM Alger to an African Confederation Cup title. A brief stint in Belgium with Sint-Truiden followed, before a big-money return to Algeria’s most decorated club, JS Kabylie, last year. Belaid is unlikely to start matches, but he is capable of slotting into defence when Algeria need to protect a lead, particularly against aerial threats. At the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations he made three appearances and scored a headed goal from a corner against Equatorial Guinea, which he then dedicated to his late father, writing online: "My first goal with the national team, on Africa's biggest stage. I felt your presence with me, dad. This goal is dedicated to you, to my family, to the coaching staff who believed in me, and to the wonderful Algerian supporters.”

data.name: Ramiz Zerrouki
data.description: In a team of twinkle-toed ball-players, Zerrouki runs against the grain. Schooled in the Ajax academy, he has mastered the game’s fundamental principles: always in the right position at the right time, with his passes tending to be safe and horizontal. He provides a balance to a team that can be unpredictable, freeing up his more forward-thinking teammates. He has earned the trust of successive Algeria coaches since his debut in 2021, but for a country that prides itself on technical flair, he isn’t a fan favourite, and often ends up being the first player to take the brunt of the blame when things go wrong. He spent last season on loan from Feyenoord back at one of his former clubs, Twente, in a bid to secure more regular game time, and his spot in this summer’s squad. It paid off.

data.name: Riyad Mahrez
data.description: Very few footballers can match what Mahrez has achieved in the game. Among his accolades, the Algeria captain boasts five Premier League titles, a Champions League winner’s medal, two FA Cups, three League Cups, Professional Footballers' Association and Confederation of African Football player of the year awards, and many other individual and club honours. The only blemish on his career for Algeria fans is that lack of a stellar display at a World Cup. In 2014 his only appearance came in a 2-1 defeat by Belgium in which he registered 37 touches, was dispossessed three times and was substituted in the 71st minute. He did not feature again as Algeria surged to the last 16. Now aged 35 and nearing the twilight of his career, he is determined to lead Algeria into the quarter-finals of the World Cup for the first time. Such an achievement would elevate his status in African footballing lore. Still, some fans argue that he no longer has the legs to justify a starting place for Algeria, and that it may already be time to hand the mantle to rising talents such as Feyenoord’s Anis Hadj Moussa.

data.name: Houssem Aouar
data.description: Premier League fans may recognise Aouar’s name from the many transfer rumours he was involved in during his final year at Lyon in 2023. In the end none of the moves came off, and instead he joined Roma, looking solid there under José Mourinho in a 3-5-2 system. His stay was brief, though: the following year he was drawn to a big-money move to Al-Ittihad, with his mentor, Karim Benzema, playing a key role in the move. Aouar, a former France youth international who was born in Lyon, switched international allegiances in 2023, saying: “It had been going through my head for a while, but I didn’t want to make an approach to Algeria myself because I was afraid of coming across as an opportunist. But when the president held his hand out to me it seemed like it was just meant to be. It means a lot to me. In all honesty, after choosing to play for France I regretted it.” With Algeria his role is to drift between the lines, receive on the half-turn, and quickly move the ball from midfield into the attackers’ feet. Don’t be surprised to see him chip in with a goal or two, either, arriving late into the box.

data.name: Amine Gouiri
data.description: Plenty of Lyon players have been dubbed “the next Karim Benzema” since the former Ballon d'Or winner rose to prominence in the mid-2000s. While all have understandably fallen short, Gouiri is perhaps the one who most closely resembles Benzema stylistically. “Karim likes to be involved in the game, by both creating chances and scoring goals,” Gouiri told Fifa.com. “Even though stats are incredibly important for a centre-forward these days, what I love is being part of the game. Combining the two is great.” With Algeria he does that to solid effect, typically starting the match as a target man before switching positions with his teammate Mohamed Amoura on the left wing. From a technical standpoint he has very few weaknesses, rarely misplacing a pass or shanking a shot. However, he does have a tendency, at times, to let the game pass him by. He will need to increase his number of touches on the ball if Algeria’s attack is to click on all cylinders in North America.

data.name: Farès Chaïbi
data.description: Terraillon, on the outskirts of Lyon, is probably best known for producing Karim Benzema. But on a visit to the suburb before the 2022 Champions League final, locals wanted to talk instead about a youngster at Toulouse named Farès Chaïbi. “He’s the full package and wants to play for Algeria,” a teenager named Younes said. A few months later, Chaïbi won Ligue 1's rookie of the year award, reflecting his eight goals in 41 appearances across all competitions. That debut season earned him a move to Eintracht Frankfurt, but in Germany he has blown hot and cold. A broad-shouldered midfielder capable of playing on the wing or through the middle in a box-to-box role, his set-piece delivery from corners and free-kicks makes him particularly valuable to Algeria; the team have steadily improved in that area under Vladimir Petkovic.

data.name: Anis Hadj Moussa
data.description: The most exciting dribbler in the side, and that isn’t an easy title to claim. A Paris native, Hadj Moussa joined Feyenoord in 2024 and has made a huge impact, becoming vice-captain and one of the Eredivisie’s most outstanding talents, regularly leading the dribbling rankings and, under the now dismissed Robin van Persie, hugely improving his end product. “It’s interesting to see how he is developing,” the former Arsenal striker said recently. “I don’t think he’ll take Riyad Mahrez’s place for Algeria because he’s a tremendous player, but Mahrez has hinted that he’ll retire after the World Cup, which would free up the spot for Hadj Moussa.” While Van Persie is probably right that Mahrez stays ahead in the pecking order, Hadj Moussa is still expected to play significant minutes, often coming on when Mahrez’s ageing legs begin to fade around the hour mark. He is another player linked with a possible move to England this summer.

data.name: Nadhir Benbouali
data.description: Benbouali has often felt that the footballing world has not been fair to him. As a teenager, he was not offered a professional contract by his hometown club ASO Chlef, despite scoring more than 30 goals in the youth leagues and despite his father working as a doctor for the senior team. "I never understood why," he told the Algerian outlet El Heddaf. "Yet, everything in life happens for a reason and I was lucky enough to sign with the best academy in Algeria, Paradou AC." At Paradou, Benbouali developed into one of the Algerian league's most prolific strikers, but a call-up to the national team remained elusive. It was only a few months ago, after finishing third in the Hungarian league's scoring charts and helping Gyori Eto secure the league title, that he finally earned his reward. Benbouali is Algeria's only true target man, a physically imposing striker who excels in aerial duels and offers a different dimension in attack. Even if he does not start, he is likely to play a role during the World Cup.

data.name: Jaouen Hadjam
data.description: Were it not for the exceptional talent of Rayan Aït-Nouri, Hadjam would be Algeria’s first-choice left-back. A product of the famed Parisian banlieues, Hadjam combines the technical assurance he developed as a youngster with mature defensive reliability. His quality on the ball means he can also do a job as a left-footed right-back; so he and Aït-Nouri could work in the same lineup. But breaking into the starting XI remains an uphill task given the competition ahead of him. Reports last winter suggested that Besiktas had reached an agreement with Young Boys for his transfer, only for the move to collapse after the player failed to agree personal terms with the Turkish side. He was at the centre of a row in 2023 when his coach at Nantes, Antoine Kombouaré, dropped him for a Ligue 1 match because he refused to break his fast during Ramadan. Kombouaré insisted: “It’s not a punishment. It’s not a controversy. I support those who fast, but on matchdays, you should not fast. I set the rules.”

data.name: Hicham Boudaoui
data.description: Riyad Mahrez may be Algeria’s most famous name, and Ibrahim Maza its most exciting prospect, but the Fennecs’ most indispensable player is definitely Hicham Boudaoui. Algeria has no shortage of inventive attacking midfielders who can dribble and score. What it often lacks is a midfielder capable of covering as much ground as Boudaoui does on a consistent basis. In one passage of play, you can catch the Bechar native shielding the backline like a traditional No 6, then surging forward in a more box-to-box role to support his wingers, and even drifting into the No 10 pocket. He regularly ranks among the top runners in Ligue 1 in terms of distance covered, averaging between 11km and 12km per match, and will be tireless in the middle for Algeria this summer. He was being linked with clubs including Brighton, Marseille and Galatasaray before the tournament; the list of suitors is likely to grow.

data.name: Rayan Aït-Nouri
data.description: You have to be brave to lunge in on Rayan Aït-Nouri when he has the ball at his feet. It’s no exaggeration to describe the France-born full-back as one of the best dribbling defenders in world football, a player who looks effortlessly comfortable carrying the ball into the final third and turning defensive sequences into attacking ones. He made the decision to represent Algeria in 2023, saying: “Algeria is my roots … When I’m in Algeria, I’m always happy; my mood changes as soon as I arrive there. The people are smiling, they’re friendly, everyone has their hands on their heart; it’s magnificent.” With the national side his form has not always been as strong as it has at club level, with mixed displays at the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations, but his pre-World Cup form at City – whom he joined from Wolves for £31m last year – was encouragingly strong. He should be a huge asset this summer.

data.name: Oussama Benbot
data.description: Benbot started the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations as Algeria's backup goalkeeper, and his tournament ended in unlikely disappointment. After the Fennecs won their opening two group-stage matches, Vladimir Petkovic rotated much of his squad for the final group game against Equatorial Guinea. Benbot, however, remained on the bench as the third-choice goalkeeper, Anthony Mandrea, was given the nod. Frustrated by the decision, the USM Alger player announced his international retirement in a statement on Instagram after the tournament. His absence, however, proved short-lived. Injuries to Luca Zidane and Melvin Mastil prompted the Algerian football federation to reach out and make clear that they still counted on him. Benbot, who had an outstanding season with USM Algiers and helped the club win the African Confederation Cup final against Zamalek, agreed to return. "Perhaps I made the decision [to retire] too quickly," he said. "I had a discussion with the president of the federation to explain my situation and what was on my mind. I thank the president of the federation because we found common ground and he understood my position."

data.name: Rafik Belghali
data.description: Belghali came dangerously close to missing this tournament. After Algeria’s quarter-final exit against Nigeria at the Africa Cup of Nations in January, he went viral for chasing the referee down the tunnel in Marrakesh, venting his frustration at the officials. He said later: “After the final whistle, I approached the referee to shake his hand. His reaction surprised me and, in the heat of the moment, I briefly followed him to ask why. There was no aggression or intention of confrontation. I do acknowledge, however, that I let the situation drag on longer than it should have.” A ban followed but, fortunately for the Paris-born wingback, the suspension was limited to the 2027 Afcon qualifiers. Over the past year he has been a key asset for Verona and the national team. While he can be exposed in one-on-one defending, his engine is relentless, constantly surging forward to provide width and overlap Riyad Mahrez, who likes to drift inside. That dynamic is a crucial part of Algeria’s attacking structure.

data.name: Mohamed Amoura
data.description: It took a 19-year-old Amoura just a few minutes to announce himself in his senior debut for ES Setif, scoring twice in the derby against their arch-rivals, CA Bordj Bou Arreridj. The double set the tone for a career defined by explosiveness. From ES Setif to Lugano to Union Saint-Gilloise and now at Wolfsburg, he has consistently risen to new challenges whether deployed on the left wing or as a false 9, with his acceleration and directness making him a constant threat. During the World Cup qualifiers Amoura led the African scoring charts with 10 goals and is now widely regarded as Algeria’s most decisive attacking weapon. In February, though, he made negative headlines when he mocked a Congolese supporter known for spending matches at the Africa Cup of Nations standing still with his right arm raised in the style of a famous statue of the country’s revered first leader, Patrice Lumumba. Amoura, who mimicked the fan then fell to the ground as though the statue had been toppled, later apologised online: "I wasn't aware of what the person or symbol in the stands represented. I simply wanted to joke around, in a good-natured way, without any ill intent or desire to provoke anyone.”

data.name: Nabil Bentaleb
data.description: Twelve years after his last World Cup appearance, Bentaleb's career has come full circle. In 2014, the then 19-year-old midfielder was a regular starter for Tottenham and had just impressed at the World Cup, with a seemingly bright future ahead of him. However, recurring disciplinary issues disrupted several spells at clubs from Spurs to Schalke, and he even found himself without a team during the 2020-21 season. Bentaleb gradually rebuilt his career in France, returning to his hometown club, Lille, where his football journey began. His greatest challenge in life came in June 2024 when he suffered a heart attack during training. After consulting with his former teammate Christian Eriksen and receiving a pacemaker, he fought his way back into the Lille side, became captain, and produced one of the best seasons of his career. "He never let go. He believed in himself, and he believed in the people who looked after him," said Lille's coach, Bruno Genesio.

data.name: Adil Boulbina
data.description: He’s an international newcomer, but Boulbina already has one standout moment for Algeria on his CV. In his first outing at the Africa Cup of Nations in January, coming on as a sub against the Democratic Republic of the Congo, he scored a stunning goal late in extra time to send Algeria through to the quarter-finals. He was trained in the famed Paradou academy, widely regarded as one of the best in Africa, and finished the 2024-25 season as the top scorer in the Algerian Ligue Professionnelle 1 with 20 goals. That form earned him a big-money move to Al-Duhail, where he reunited with the former Algeria coach Djamel Belmadi. He is unlikely to start this summer, but his directness and ability to change games make him a valuable option off the bench.

data.name: Ramy Bensebaini
data.description: Not many centre-halves have nutmegs and chipped finishes in their highlight reels, but Bensebaini is one of those. A regular for Borussia Dortmund, he is Algeria’s standout defender. Gifted with a wand-like left foot, he is central to Algeria’s buildup play from the back. Beyond his composure on the ball, he brings intensity, pace and physical presence, although he can be prone to lapses in concentration. That vulnerability was evident this year in the Champions League playoff tie against Atalanta, where he was repeatedly at fault before conceding a penalty and being sent off. Just over a decade ago he was on trial at Arsenal, but it came to nothing, due to paperwork. "I spent time there, a month on trial, in 2015," Bensebaini told Ouest France in 2019. "I was training with the first team and I was playing matches with the reserves. I had spoken several times with Arsène Wenger. In the end, five coaches decided my future. Four of them gave me the thumbs-up and would have taken me, but one of them said it was impossible to sign me, because I didn't have a European passport and didn't have enough international caps."

data.name: Ibrahim Maza
data.description: Aged just 20, Maza is clearly going places. A fine attacking talent, he excels at drifting into pockets of space, receiving the ball on the half-turn. His low centre of gravity and powerful lower body allow him to ride challenges and show off the technical quality that sets him apart, with incisive passes and fearless dribbles. Since arriving at Bayer Leverkusen he has drawn plenty of comparisons to Florian Wirtz. Not that he is pleased about it. “I don’t like being compared like that,” Maza told the club’s website. “He became the most expensive signing in the Premier League, and I hope I can be at his level some day, but I’m not there yet.” Born in Berlin, he played for Germany up to under-20 level before declaring for Algeria last year. It was a calculated decision. “It’s purely for sporting reasons, because it aligns better with my footballing future,” he said. “In the German national team, the competition for my position is extremely fierce, so it's tough to break into the starting XI or find space in that squad. From a sporting perspective, representing Algeria gives me a greater chance to play in the World Cup and compete at the highest level. That's why I made this decision with full confidence."

data.name: Luca Zidane
data.description: “If I called him up, it’s because he deserves it,” Vladimir Petkovic told the Algerian press after a few eyebrows were raised by Luca Zidane’s first appearance in the national squad last year, the manager denying that the young keeper’s famous dad had influenced his decision. During the Africa Cup of Nations, Zinedine Zidane attended three of Algeria’s matches to support his son and, at times, it did feel like he overshadowed Luca’s impressive performances in goal. The Granada keeper, who played for France at various youth levels before declaring for Algeria, isn’t the tallest No 1 at 1.83m (6ft), but he is a good communicator, and his distribution is excellent, which allows the side to play out from the back with composure. Whenever he makes a save, Algerian fans chant: “Zizou, Zizou, Zizou.”

data.name: Yacine Titraoui
data.description: “The Algerian Verratti” is how Titraoui is often seen by north African fans. No Algerian player is better at sitting in front of the defence and dictating play with precise, progressive passing than the Charleroi midfielder. His pass completion rate hovers around 85%, and he excels at reading the game and making timely interceptions. Another trait he shares with Verratti, though, is a tendency to pick up bookings. After two full seasons with Charleroi, he will be hoping that a positive World Cup showing this summer could secure a move to one of Europe’s elite leagues. In the months before the tournament he was linked with several clubs in England including Leeds, Sunderland and Wolves.

data.name: Farès Ghedjemis
data.description: Ghedjemis enjoyed a breakthrough campaign this season, scoring 15 goals in 37 matches from the wing as he helped Frosinone secure promotion back to Serie A after a three-year absence. He is regarded as one of the standout players in Serie B and has generated considerable excitement among supporters in advance of next season. At first glance, Ghedjemis appears to fit a familiar mould for Algeria. A left-footed winger born in the suburbs of Paris who operates primarily on the right flank, he belongs to a generation heavily influenced by Riyad Mahrez's style of play. However, there is one key difference. Unlike the many gifted Algerian wingers who prefer receiving the ball to their feet, Ghedjemis is constantly looking to make runs in behind opposition defences. That attribute is presumably what convinced Petkovic to select him ahead of other creative wide players such as Ilan Kebbal, Badredine Bouanani and Mohamed Bachir Belloumi.

data.name: Samir Chergui
data.description: It didn’t take Algeria fans long to work out what Chergui would bring to the national side when he came off the bench for his debut last year. No nerves, no hesitation – just an all-action display featuring a heated running battle with an opponent. Afterwards, the goalkeeper Alexis Guendouz captioned footage on social media: “Chergui, my soldier.” In a squad characterised by technical flair, neon boots and expensive hairdos, Chergui is a balding old-school no-frills defender who just gets on with the job. He has matured since his early days at Auxerre, where he was released after a series of disciplinary incidents. He kept working and was given a second chance by Paris FC, and has fully repaid their faith. He shares his stories with the club’s younger players. “I tell them to appreciate how lucky they are to be there. My example should serve as a lesson for them."
